observations.recommendation:
1.11 We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to:
a. Instruct the Municipal Accountant and OIC-GSO to work
closely on the preparation, review, reconciliation, and updating
of their respective reports/records. Thereafter, make the
necessary adjustments on any noted discrepancies to present
fairly the PPE balances in the financial statements; and
b. Avail of the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts as provided in
COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020, to finally
establish PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence,
condition, and accountability.

2.6 We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to:
a. Direct the Municipal Accountant to:
• Perform the review of the Real Property Tax Receivables
account in coordination with the Municipal Treasurer to
determine the variances for corrections/adjustments;
• Conduct regular reconciliation of the Real Property Tax
Receivables balance with the MTO; and
• Identify the discrepancies between the Real Property Tax
Receivables and its reciprocal account, reconcile the same and
make the necessary adjustments, if warranted, to bring the
accounts to their correct balances;
b. Require the Municipal Treasurer to:
• Submit the certified list of taxpayers and the amount due and
collectible for the year or equivalent records for receivable
recognition to the Municipal Accountant in the first week of
the start of the year for the timely and proper recognition of
the Real Property Tax Receivables;
• Coordinate with the OIC-Municipal Accountant in the review
of the Real Property Tax Receivables account to determine
the variances for corrections/adjustments; and
• Conduct regular reconciliation of the Real Property Tax
Receivables balance with the OIC, Municipal Accountant; and
c. Instruct the Municipal Assessor to reconcile their records for
Real Property Tax with the records of the Municipal Accountant
and the Municipal Treasurer to ensure the accuracy and
integrity of information across all responsible units.

3.11 We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to:
a. Direct the Inventory Committee to perform the following:
• Faithful conduct of the physical count of inventories on a
semestral basis and preparation of the RPCIs for all
inventory accounts;
• Reconciliation of all inventory accounts before the
submission of the RPCI to the Audit Team;
• Inclusion in the Semi-Expendable Property Cards and RPCI
essential information about the property custodian, location
and condition of the semi-expendable properties;
• Strictly comply with the set deadlines in the submission of
RPCI; and
• Facilitate the immediate tagging of all high-valued semi-
expendable property;
b. Direct the Municipal Accountant to:
• Strictly apply the Perpetual Inventory Method in recording
the inventories;
• Prepare and maintain the ledgers for all inventory accounts
including the semi-expendable property;
• Record the issuances of the semi-expendable property as
semi-expendable expenses;
• Ensure that all recorded expenses for the issuances of
inventories are supported with RSMIs/SSMIs; and
• Reconcile their records with those of the GSO and other
inventory custodians and effect immediately the necessary
adjustments or corrections in the books to bring the
inventory accounts to their correct balances; and
c. Require the GSO and other inventory custodians to:
• Prepare and regularly update the stock cards/semi-
expendable property cards of all inventory accounts;
• Prepare and submit all RIS and RSMI/SSMIs of all issued
inventories to the Municipal Accountant; and
• Reconcile their records with the accounting records and effect
the necessary corrections in their stock cards and
semi-expendable property cards.

4.8 We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to require the
Municipal Accountant to:
a. Conduct immediately the analysis of the Due to National
Government Agencies and Due to LGUs accounts and identify the
true nature of the transactions for the proper disposition of the
errors and deficiencies;
b. Cause the immediate preparation of reclassification entries for the
misclassified accounts; and
c. Ensure the proper and complete relevant information in the
supporting schedules of the Due to National Government Agencies
and Due to LGUs accounts.

5.7 We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the
Municipal Accountant to immediately:
a. Conduct a comprehensive review of the Due from Other Funds
and Due to Other Funds accounts to identify and locate all
discrepancies; and
b. Implement the necessary adjustments to ensure these accounts
reflect their accurate balances at all times.

6.11 We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the
Municipal Accountant to:
a. Re-evaluate the previous years’ transactions, exert more effort to
secure the documents supporting the prior years' reconciling
items, and cause their immediate recording, particularly those that
remained in the BRS for an extended period;
b. Require the Municipal Treasurer to submit immediately the
documents for the unrecorded check and cash deposits so that
entries could be made to the proper fund;
c. Coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer in sending letters to the
payees of stale checks requesting them to present the previously
issued checks for cancellation and reissuance of a new negotiable
instrument; and
d. Revise the BRS for LBP Account No. 0032-1101-18 to transfer the
bank reconciling items to book reconciling items.

7.12 We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to require the:
a. Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to periodically
reconcile their records to determine the accurate Cash on Hand
balances;
b. Municipal Accountant to cause the immediate preparation of
appropriate adjusting entries upon identifying variances; and
c. Acting Municipal Treasurer to ensure that all collections and
deposits are accurately recorded in their respective cash books and
that the proper procedure for fund transfers between bank accounts
is strictly followed. This includes issuing a check from the account
where the full Real Property Tax was originally deposited and
subsequently depositing it into the appropriate fund account.

8.7 We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the
Municipal Accountant to prepare immediately the reclassification
entries to bring the affected accounts to their correct balances.

9.11 We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the
Municipal Accountant to:
a. Prepare the detailed supporting schedule for Guaranty/Security
Deposits Payable; and
b. Prepare the Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to reclassify the
retention money from Other Payables to Guaranty/Security
Deposits Payable.

10.10 We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the
Municipal Treasurer to establish comprehensive standby plans with
well-defined strategies to address any obstacles and ensure successful
collection for the adequate funding of all the Municipality’s
programs and projects.

11.7 We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the
Municipal Accountant to prioritize the retrieval of reports and
documents pertaining to these unaccounted balances of AP and
prepare immediately the adjusting entries to bring the AP to its
correct balance at any given period.

12.9 We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to:
a. Require the Human Resource Management Officer III to:
• Regularly update the leave cards to facilitate the monitoring
of leave credits and other personnel benefits;
• Determine the cost or money value of the accumulated leave
credits of incumbent personnel and submit them to the OIC,
Municipal Accountant; and
• Assigned additional personnel to assist in the updating of
leave cards; and
b. Direct the Municipal Accountant to:
• Start accruing earned leave benefits upon determination of
the cost or money value of the accumulated leave credits,
starting with the amounts to be monetized for the year and
the terminal leave benefits of those employees with intent to
retire for the upcoming year; and
• Disclose in the Notes to FS the impending retroactive
recognition of the accumulated credits of the Municipal
personnel, providing all necessary details and a recognition
schedule.

13.8 We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to:
a. Direct the Municipal Accountant to:
• Send letters or notices to the concerned suppliers or
contractors informing them of their retention money due for
refund, and asking them to submit letter requests for the
refund;
• Commence retrieving or gathering of documents of the
suppliers or contractors whose deliveries of goods/rendition of
services are beyond the warranty periods, with full or final
acceptance of deliveries and payments, serving as supporting
documents for the refund; and
• In coordination with the Municipal Treasurer, process the
payments for the return of retention money; and
b. Install a monitoring mechanism on retention money for the
timely return of these securities to suppliers.

14.14 We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to:
a. Direct the Municipal Engineer/OIC-Municipal Planning
Development Officer to:
• Strictly follow the schedules for development project
implementation to ensure the timely delivery of services to the
public;
• Adopt periodic review on the feasibility of implementation of
the various PPAs of the 20 percent DF to promptly determine
whether the corresponding allocations can be reprogrammed
to other development projects;
• Coordinate with the Municipal Budget Officer in ensuring
that savings realized from prior years’ development projects
are reprogrammed in the succeeding year; and
• Generate a comprehensive 20% Development Fund
Utilization Report, detailing the utilization of funds for
projects for current/continuing appropriations and
reprogrammed savings; and
b. Direct the Municipal Accountant to assist the Municipal
Engineer in the preparation of the 20% Development Fund
Utilization Report, and ensure that the project start-up and
target completion dates of PPAs are included therein.

15.9 We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the
GAD Focal Person to ensure the:
a. Submission of the DILG-endorsed GPB and GAD AR to the
Audit Team within the prescribed period; and
b. Optimum utilization of GAD funds through the implementation
of all identified programs/projects/activities to attain the GAD
objectives of pursuing gender equality and empowerment.

16.7 We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the GAD
Focal Person to:
a. Ensure that the GPB and GAD AR performance indicators and
targets indicate the specific number of male and female
beneficiaries of the GAD Programs, Projects, and Activities
(PPAs); and
b. Prepare the GPB to include projects and activities that address
gender issues and objectives and discontinue including activities
which are not GAD-related and responsive. The budgets and
reports which are not GAD-related must be allocated and
reported separately.

17.13 We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the
LDRRMO II to:
a. Prepare annually the LDRRMFIP for the DRRM program that
presents the 30 percent allocation for the QRF in-lumpsum and
the allocation for the disaster mitigation, prevention and
preparedness with the details as to projects and activities to be
funded; and
b. Include under a separate caption, list of projects and activities
charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years, as well
as to the continuing appropriations.

18.8 We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the
LDRRMO II to optimize the use of the LDRRMF by implementing
identified Programs, Projects, and Activities in alignment with the
prescribed guidelines for fund utilization, and to oversee and closely
monitor the timely execution of the related PPAs.

19.9 We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to:
a. Direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare Disbursement and
Journal Entry Vouchers for the reversion of the unexpended
balance of the LDRRMF-STF amounting to ₱4.418 million; and
b. Instruct the Acting Municipal Treasurer to issue a check to
transfer the same to the General Fund account.

20.9 We recommended and the Municipal Mayor and the LSB agreed to
ensure that the SEF plans and budget are effectively and efficiently
executed and optimally expended.

21.13 We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the:
a. OIC-GSO to officially transfer the above Property, Plant and
Equipment to the Department of Education, by securing
authority from the Sangguniang Bayan by way of a resolution,
to authorize the Municipal Mayor to execute a Deed of Donation
or a Memorandum of Agreement. A copy of the journal entry
voucher (JEV) prepared by the Department of Education
recording the receipt of properties transferred by the LGU
should also be submitted to the Audit Team.
b. Municipal Accountant to periodically determine and
consistently provide the correct depreciation for all LGU
properties to ensure fair presentation of depreciation and related
accounts in the financial statements.

22.7 We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the
Municipal Engineer to:
a. Ensure the submission of a comprehensive list of planned and
ongoing Municipality’s GPPA to the Audit Team at the start of
each year; and
b. Prepare the infrastructure monitoring reports in the required
format/template containing the essential information about the
GPPAs implementation in reference to COA Circular
No. 2013-004.

23.8 We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to:
a. Direct the HRMO to:
• Formulate the hiring policies for the Job Order/Contractual
personnel, ensure the conduct of manpower needs assessment
and staffing patterns, and prepare the standardized duties
and responsibilities for each designation; and
• Prepare the payroll certification for approval of the
Municipal Mayor that the services rendered are not
performed by plantillla personnel; and
b. Direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to require the submission
of the payroll certification before the processing of the payments
for Job Order/Contractual personnel.

24.7 We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the
Municipal Accountant to:
a. Send demand letters to concerned employees for the immediate
liquidation of their cash advances; and
b. Henceforth, ensure the timely liquidation of all cash advances
pursuant to Section 89 of PD No. 1445 and Section 5.1 of COA
Circular No. 97-002

25.6 We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the
Municipal Accountant to prepare immediately the journal entry
voucher (JEV) to reclassify the cash advances for employees’ salaries
and benefits amounting to ₱11.389 million from Advances to Special
Disbursing Officer to Advances for Payroll.

26.13 We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to:
a. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
• Provide the OIC-GSO with the list of properties that have
exceeded their estimated useful life; and
• Disclose the remaining unserviceable properties for disposal
and their computed impairment losses in the Notes to
Financial Statements until the RCA is updated.
b. Direct the OIC-GSO to:
• Identify unserviceable properties from the Municipal
Accountant's list of properties that have exceeded their
estimated useful life;
• Prepare corresponding IIRUPs and distribute copies to the
Disposal Committee and Municipal Accountant; and
• Evaluate all remaining PPEs to determine their condition for
serviceability and potential disposal.

27.9 We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the
Municipal Assessor to initiate the processing of the transfer of titles
of the remaining lots to the name of the Municipality.

28.12 We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the:
a. Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to:
• Exert effort to retrieve the documents and review the
unreconciled prior years’ balances;
• Evaluate the need for those unexpended and non-moving fund
balances, and if determined that it is no longer needed, they
must be remitted to the source agencies as required under
existing rules and regulations or, otherwise, request for the
appropriate authority to use the said funds; and
• Ensure that funds received from source agencies are
supported with duly signed Memorandum of Agreement and
related documents, before allowing any disbursements and
ensure that funds are utilized only for their intended
purposes;
b. Project proponents to thoroughly plan projects, consider their
locations and the prevailing peace and order conditions, and
ensure that their execution aligns with the schedules to guarantee
timely implementation.

29.9 We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the
Municipal Accountant and Acting Municipal Treasurer to render
their accounts and submit for audit those unsubmitted DVs to avoid
possible suspension or disallowance, and to strictly adhere to the
timeframes provided in COA Circular No. 2009-006 and other
pertinent issuances.

30.7 We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the
Municipal Accountant to:
a. Implement strictly the mandated net take home pay of the
employees based on Section 56 of the 2024 GAA; and
b. Devise monitoring and control mechanisms in the approval of
loans of the employees to ensure that the employee has take home
pay within what is required by the GAA.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the:

Inventory Committee to properly plan and conduct the physical count of all PPEs and ensure timely and complete submission of RPCPPE to the Office of the Auditor; and

b.	OIC-General Services Officer and the OIC-Municipal Accountant to work together in reconciling and updating both their records. Thereafter, make the necessary adjustments on any discrepancies noted.

Likewise, we recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to avail of the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts as provided in COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 to finally establish PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require:

The OIC, Municipal Accountant to summarize all reconciling items without available supporting documents and references, and submit the same with a query to the COA Government Accountancy Sector (GAS) for its appropriate accounting treatment and remedies to clean the LGU’s books;

The Acting Municipal Treasurer to request necessary bank documents to support reconciling items requiring adjustments to the Cash in Bank Account of the OIC-Municipal Accountant with exact description and reference; and

The Acting Municipal Treasurer and OIC-Municipal Accountant to undertake reconciliation of the cashbook balances with accounting records regularly in order for necessary corrections and adjustments to be taken up in the books in a timely manner.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer and other employee in-charge on the submission of financial documents to immediately submit the aforementioned disbursement vouchers with supporting documents, and to adhere strictly to the timeframes on the rendition of accounts pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-006 and other pertinent issuances.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the Municipal Assessor to secure independent appraisal report/s on the purchased lot from the Appraisal Committee of the Iloilo Provincial Government or from any of the authorized appraisers, and submit said report/s to the Office of the Auditor as soon as possible.



We also recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the OIC – Municipal Accountant to cause the submission of the documents relevant to the purchase of land, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 to establish the validity and propriety of the transaction.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the OIC, Municipal Accountant to record in the inventory and semi-expendable property accounts the purchase of supplies and semi-expendable equipment. 

We also recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the General Services Officer to prepare a Report of Semi-Expendable Property Issued and a Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued weekly, then submit the same together with the ICS and RIS to the OIC, Municipal Accountant.

We further recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the concerned officials to submit the lacking documents listed for each disbursement and direct the Bids and Awards Committee and the Municipal Accountant to henceforth see to it that supporting documents are complete and proper before payments are made.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to:

Verify the validity of payables aged two years or more and send confirmation letters for balances with identified claimants or creditors. Valid claims shall be paid, otherwise, cause the reversion of the payables to the unappropriated surplus of the general fund; and

Exert effort to retrieve the documents of unidentified payables or those without details in the subsidiary ledgers to properly classify the amounts recorded.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to instruct the Municipal Planning and Development Council, Municipal Budget Officer and Department Heads to evaluate the necessity of the identified priority PPAs, which to date remained unimplemented. Otherwise, reprogram the same to much needed PPAs in accordance with Section 3.2.2 of DBM-DOF-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 1, and to henceforth properly plan and execute all procurement activities pertaining thereto to ensure the timely implementation and completion in accordance with Section 3.2.3 of the same circular, so as to ensure the judicious delivery of services to the constituents.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the MDRRMO and the OIC - Municipal Accountant to prepare and submit the Report on Sources and Utilization of the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund on a monthly basis, on or before the 15th day after the end of each month pursuant to Section 12 of RA 10121 and Item 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 to facilitate the timely review of the utilization of the LDRRMF.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the MDRRMC to prepare and submit the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund Investment Plan (LDRRMFIP) indicating the detailed PPAs to be funded, and include the list of projects and activities charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years under a separate caption for ease of monitoring of project implementation and evaluation.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the LDRRMO to:

Maximize the utilization of the LDRRMF by the operation of the identified Programs, Projects and Activities congruent with the guidelines set forth on the utilization of the fund, and oversee and closely monitor the implementation of relative PPAs;

Refrain from utilizing DRRM funds for any other purpose other than those for which they have been appropriated; and

Submit the documentary evidences to establish the legal identities of all the recipients of cash and relief assistance, and the qualifying documents to determine their eligibility thereto.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to take the following courses of action:

Reorganize its Solid Waste Management Board to include the President of the Association of Barangay Councils in the Municipality as well as representatives from the recycling, manufacturing or packaging industries, and from NGOs that are related to environment protection to conform with the membership requirement under RA 9003 and to optimize the involvement of environment and other industry sectors in solid waste management activities of the Municipality;

Direct the MENR Officer to furnish the OIC-Municipal Accountant with the relevant documents pertaining to the Ecological Solid Waste Management Equipment received from the Environmental Management Bureau (EMB) totaling ₱998,000.00 to facilitate the recording thereof in the LGU books; and

Coordinate with the Local Sanggunian, as the local taxing authority, to enact an updated tax ordinance or Local Revenue Code, with provisions to collect garbage fees from occupants of residential houses, apartments and flats so that the revenue generating potential of the LGU’s Solid Waste Management Program is optimized, and will provide the LGU with the means to attain a measure of financial autonomy.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the concerned officials to submit the lacking documents listed for each program, project, and activity and direct the Bids and Awards Committee and the OIC-Municipal Accountant to henceforth see to it that procurement documents are complete and proper before payments are made to contractors.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the GFPS to prepare the Municipality’s GAD Agenda for the three-year period as required under Item C2 par. 1 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 to establish the Municipality’s strategic framework and plan on gender mainstreaming, women’s empowerment and gender equality which should be the basis in the preparation of the annual GPB.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer and other employee in-charge on the submission of financial documents to immediately submit the aforementioned disbursement vouchers with supporting documents, and to adhere strictly to the timeframes on the rendition of accounts pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-006 and other pertinent issuances.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to impose the payment of liquidated damages to contractors with delayed project completion in accordance with Section 8, Annex E of RA 9184.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the concerned officials to submit the lacking documents listed for each project and direct the Bids and Awards Committee and the OIC-Municipal Accountant to henceforth see to it that procurement documents are complete and proper before payments are made to contractors.
We recommended and Management agreed to submit photographs to provide proof of implementation and to support the existence of the projects.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct concerned officials and employees to submit:

The necessary documents to validate the issuances of various medicines and medical supplies amounting to ₱1.430 million. Henceforth, the issuances of purchased drugs and medicines should be duly accounted and properly documented to justify the recording thereof as expenses;

The Acceptance and Inspection Reports of consumable and perishable items to this Office 24 hours from acceptance of the items delivered; and

The documentary requirements mentioned in Section 9.1.3.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 to ensure that items procured originated from legitimate sources and are effective as well as genuine. Henceforth, procurement of drugs and medicines should be duly supported with the requisite documents.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct: 

The BAC and its Secretariat to submit the lacking documents, as enumerated, to the Audit Team and warrant the completeness of supporting documents that need to be prepared / submitted in the procurement stages of projects in accordance with the provisions of DILG MC No. 2020-088 and RA No. 9184 and its Revised IRR; and

The OIC-Municipal Accountant to submit the duly approved notice of time extension and suspension of work, if any, otherwise, impose the payment of liquidated damages to the contractor with delayed project completion in accordance with Section 8, Annex E of RA 9184.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the BAC and its Secretariat to submit the lacking documents, as enumerated, to the Audit Team and warrant the completeness of supporting documents that need to be prepared /submitted in the procurement stages of projects in accordance with the provisions of LBC No. 119 s. 2019, DILG MC No. 2020-088, and RA No. 9184 and its Revised IRR.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require concerned officials and employees to submit the lacking documents in accordance with Section 3.f, Article A, Chapter XVI of Ordinance No. 2014-06

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to:

Create a Disposal Committee tasked with the disposal of unserviceable properties;


Direct the General Services Officer to identify the unserviceable properties subject to inspection and appraisal in order to expedite their disposal; and

Direct the Disposal Committee to follow the disposal procedures and accomplish the forms to be used in reporting disposable supplies and property.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer and other employee in-charge on the submission of financial documents to immediately submit the aforementioned disbursement vouchers with supporting documents, and to adhere strictly to the timeframes on the rendition of accounts pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-006 and other pertinent issuances.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to:

Instruct the BAC or duly authorized procuring unit to require the supplier a copy of its Registration Certificate/License to operate as rice dealer/seller issued by the NFA otherwise, provide a justification as to the efficiency of negotiating from the supplier instead of directly negotiating from the NFA or a NFA licensed rice dealer;

Instruct the BAC to provide a justification as to the acceptance of the OSS submitted by the supplier despite the apparent deficiencies noted in the document as well as in the Contract/PO; and

Direct the end-user unit to submit a certification stating the type/variety of rice that was received from the supplier.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the MSWDO to submit the guidelines used in the eligibility check conducted including the supporting documents required to be submitted by the target recipients/households in order to qualify for the financial assistance.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the concerned officials and employees to immediately stop the granting of additional benefits to Job Order personnel to avoid personal liability for irregular disbursements, and require the refund of disbursed funds amounting to ₱20,000.00.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to look into the possible collection and /or liquidation of the dormant receivables account of the LGU. In the event that collection and/or liquidation of the said accounts could no longer be accomplished, filing a request for write-off of dormant receivables is in order.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to return/remit to the Source Agency any unexpended amount of fund transfer after project completion pursuant to COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive and the Local School Board agreed to fully implement the programs, projects and activities identified in the Annual Budget to attain the objectives of SEF of providing financial support to intended beneficiaries as provided in RA 5447 and RA 7160 and in harmony with DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, series of 2017.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to convene with the Local School Board and prepare the three-year program for the strategic prioritization of policies in the allocation and use of the Special Education Fund.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the concerned officials and employees to expedite the titling of the said lot in the name of the LGU to document ownership over the property and to avoid the payment of unnecessary penalties that may be imposed by regulatory bodies.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to maintain a special account for the 20% MDF and prepare the required financial statements as enumerated in the above mentioned guidelines in order to provide a comprehensive information as to the extent of implementation of programs/projects undertaken by the Municipality that was sourced from its development fund.

We recommended and Management agreed that all Municipal Officials and Employees refund the PEI amounting to ₱3,048,467.58 in excess of its PS limitation granted to its employees since it is contrary to the provisions of Section 325 (a) of RA 7160 and Local Budget Circular No. 98.
We recommended and Management agreed to refund the excess amount of Productivity Enhancement Incentive for CY 2013 totaling ₱3,274,000.00 paid to the Municipal officials and employees.

We recommended and Management agreed to submit all contracts, purchase orders and the like entered into, irrespective of the amount, to the Auditor within five (5) working days upon execution and issuance thereof.

We recommended and the Management agreed to direct the:
Inventory Committee to continue the conduct of the physical count of all PPEs to establish the existence, validity and accuracy of PPEs and ensure timely and complete submission of the RPCPPE to the Office of the Auditor; and
OIC, Municipal Accountant and OIC, GSO to work closely in the reconciliation of PPE balances and make necessary adjustments to arrive at equal balances in their respective records.
1.12 	Likewise, we recommended and the Management agreed to avail of the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts as provided in COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 to finally establish PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability.

3.10 	We recommended and the Management agreed to officially transfer the above Property, Plant and Equipment to the Department of Education, by securing authority from the Sangguniang Bayan by way of resolution, to authorize the Municipal Mayor to execute a Deed of Donation or a Memorandum of Agreement. A copy of the journal entry voucher (JEV) prepared by the Department of Education recording the receipt of properties transferred by the LGU should also be submitted to COA.
3.11 	We also recommended and the Management agreed to instruct the OIC, Municipal Accountant to determine and consistently provide the correct depreciation for all LGU properties periodically to ensure fair presentation of depreciation and related accounts in the financial statements.

4.11 	We recommended and the Management agreed to require:
The OIC, Municipal Accountant to summarize all reconciling items without available supporting documents and references and submit the same with a query to the COA Government Accountancy Sector (GAS) for its appropriate accounting treatment; and
The Acting, Municipal Treasurer and OIC-Municipal Accountant to undertake reconciliation of the cashbook balances with accounting records regularly in order for necessary corrections and adjustments to be taken up in the books in a timely manner.

5.7 	We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the OIC, Municipal Accountant and Acting Municipal Treasurer to render their accounts and submit for audit those unsubmitted disbursement vouchers to avoid possible suspension or disallowance, and to strictly adhere to the timeframes provided in COA Circular No. 2009-006 and other pertinent issuances.

6.35 	We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the project proponents to thoroughly plan projects, consider their locations, and ensure that their execution aligns with the schedules to guarantee timely implementation.

Otherwise, reprogram the same together with the savings generated from completed PPAs to much-needed PPAs in accordance with Section 3.2.2 of DBM-DOF-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 1, and to henceforth properly plan and execute all procurement activities pertaining thereto to ensure the timely implementation and completion in accordance with Section 3.2.3 of the same circular, so as to ensure the judicious delivery of services to the constituents.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and officials concerned ensure to:

Expedite the inventory-taking in accordance with the following prescribed procedures:

Property Identification and Coding

Government Property shall be properly labeled and identified by the Inventory Committee. Inventory labels of special materials shall contain among others the individual property number to be provided by the LGU concerned. These numbers shall be uniformly printed in India ink, to achieve permanence and best results, and shall be posted on prominent but secure portion of the property for easy identification.

Acknowledgment Receipt for Equipment (ARE)

An officer or employee who receives and is in possession or physical control of the property shall be issued an ARE. Such ARE shall be renewed every three (3) years or every time there is a change in accountability as required by NGAS, Volume II.

Inventory Reports

The report on the physical count of property, plant and equipment shall be prepared on the format prescribed by the NGAS, Volume II. The report shall be submitted to the Auditor concerned not later than January 31 of each year for the property, plant and equipment.

Reconcile the inventory reports with the accounting and property records.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct:

The Municipal Accountant and the General Services Officer to prepare and maintain PPE ledger cards and property cards for each type of PPE and perform a reconciliation thereof;

The inventory committee to establish the existence and condition of properties owned by the municipality thru the conduct of physical count of PPE in compliance with Section 124 of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs, Volume I and Section 156 of COA Circular No. 92-386 which prescribes the Rules and Regulations on Supply and Property Management in the LGUs and to perform the inventory-taking in accordance with the following prescribed procedures:

	Creation of Inventory Committee
	
A committee shall be formed to take charge of the physical inventory-taking, consisting of the representative of the local chief executive as chairman, the general services officer, and the supply accountable officer of the department or office concerned.
	
	Property Identification and Coding

Government Property shall be properly labeled and identified by the Inventory Committee. Inventory labels of special materials shall contain among others the individual property number to be provided by the LGU concerned. These numbers shall be uniformly printed in India ink, to achieve permanence and best results, and shall be posted on prominent but secure portion of the property for easy identification.

	Acknowledgment Receipt for Equipment (ARE)

An officer or employee who receives and is in possession or physical control of the property shall be issued an ARE. Such ARE shall be renewed every three (3) years or every time there is a change in accountability as required by NGAS, Volume II.

	Inventory Reports

The report on the physical count of property, plant and equipment shall be prepared on the format prescribed by the NGAS, Volume II. The report shall be submitted to the Auditor concerned not later than January 31 of each year for the property, plant and equipment.

The officials concerned to reconcile the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment with the Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Cards and the Property Cards.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct:
The Municipal Accountant and the General Services Officer to prepare and maintain PPE ledger cards and property cards for each type of PPE and perform a reconciliation thereof;
The inventory committee to establish the existence and condition of properties owned by the municipality thru the conduct of physical count of PPE in compliance with Section 124 of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs, Volume I and Section 156 of COA Circular No. 92-386 which prescribes the Rules and Regulations on Supply and Property Management in the LGUs and to perform the inventory-taking in accordance with the following prescribed procedures:
Creation of Inventory Committee
A committee shall be formed to take charge of the physical inventory-taking, consisting of the representative of the local chief executive as chairman, the general services officer, and the supply accountable officer of the department or office concerned.
Property Identification and Coding
Government Property shall be properly labeled and identified by the Inventory Committee. Inventory labels of special materials shall contain among others the individual property number to be provided by the LGU concerned. These numbers shall be uniformly printed in India ink, to achieve permanence and best results, and shall be posted on prominent but secure portion of the property for easy identification.
Acknowledgment Receipt for Equipment (ARE)
An officer or employee who receives and is in possession or physical control of the property shall be issued an ARE. Such ARE shall be renewed every three (3) years or every time there is a change in accountability as required by NGAS, Volume II.
Inventory Reports
The report on the physical count of property, plant and equipment shall be prepared on the format prescribed by the NGAS, Volume II. The report shall be submitted to the Auditor concerned not later than January 31 of each year for the property, plant and equipment.
The officials concerned to reconcile the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment with the Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Cards and the Property Cards.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant to prepare and submit immediately the bank reconciliation statements from January to December 2017 for all funds, and effect adjusting/correcting entries, if necessary, and henceforth submit the same, on a monthly basis, within the reglementary period, to the Auditor’s Office for audit purposes.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to submit the Monthly Pre-Closing Trial Balance for all funds and the Status of Appropriations, Allotments and Obligation for both the current and continuing appropriations for January to November 2017, as well as the Trial Balances with the schedule of subsidiary ledger balances for the 1st to 3rd Quarters of CY 2017 for verification of the municipality’s accounts and financial transactions.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the concerned officials and employees to expedite the titling of the said lot in the name of the LGU to document ownership over the property and to avoid the payment of unnecessary penalties that may be imposed by regulatory bodies.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Require the Municipal Accountant to record the fund transfer following the prescribed accounting entry:
Due from LGUs			P160,000.00
Cash in Bank				P160,000.00
Make the necessary adjustments to properly recognize fund transfers to the Municipality of Miagao following the adjusting entry as shown:
Due from LGUs			P160,000.00
Prior Period Adjustment		P160,000.00
Require the Municipality of Miagao to submit a report on the implementation and utilization of the funds received pursuant to Section 3.1.3 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and the Local School Board fully implement the programs, projects and activities identified in the Annual Budget to attain the objectives of SEF of providing financial support to intended beneficiaries as provided in RA 5447 and RA 7160 and in harmony with DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, series of 2017.

We recommended that the municipality’s LCPC prepare the Annual Work and Financial Plan, Local Development Plan for Children and the Local Investment Plan for Children in accordance with the guidelines above, to serve as guide for an efficient and effective implementation of its programs/projects/activities. It should also be included in the Annual Investment Program of the municipality to ensure its funding.
We also recommended that the council prepare the Annual Accomplishment Report to enable proper evaluation and verification of the implementation of its planned programs and activities as embodied in its Annual Work and Financial Plan.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to return/remit to the Source Agency any unexpended amount of fund transfer after project completion pursuant to COA Circular 94-013 dated December 13, 1994.
Direct the Municipal Accountant to strictly comply with documentary requirements by securing MOA/Trust Agreements and approved project proposals to form part of supporting documents depending on the nature of transaction in the implementation and liquidation of fund transfers as provided in Section 3.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.
Direct the Focal Person and Project Proponent Department Head to implement immediately without delay the funded programs of various NGAs to attain the program’s objectives by creating a Project Monitoring Committee to oversee all fund transfers for BuB Projects, as well as for Salintubig and ADM projects.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and officials concerned impose liquidated damages to the suppliers in the total amount of P883,148.75 for failure on their part to deliver the goods procured within the specified contract of time, and submit an explanation or justification on the non-imposition thereof despite the absence of a valid time extension, in accordance with Annex D of The 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA 9184.
We also recommended that an explanation regarding the different dates noted in the Acceptance and Inspection Report and the supplier’s sales invoice for the procurement of various hospital equipment be submitted to this Office.
We further recommended that the hospital equipment currently stored at the covered gym be moved to a more secure area to ensure that loss and further deterioration are prevented.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and officials concerned prioritize only programs/projects/activities which are feasible to be implemented within the time frame set in the Annual Investment Plan of the municipality.
We also recommended that there should be proper monitoring of the progress of the program/project implementation so as to ensure that it is accomplished in a timely manner for the constituents to enjoy the benefits that can be derived therefrom.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the concerned officials to submit an explanation or justification on the non-imposition of liquidated damages for projects that were noted to have incurred delays in implementation/completion, but were paid in full despite the absence of a valid time extension, in accordance with Annex E of The 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA 9184.
We likewise recommended that future similar transactions should be supported with duly approved requests for time extensions which should be attached to the disbursement vouchers to enable proper validation thereof.
We also recommended that the LCE direct all officials concerned to include in the future contracts, the duration within which to complete the project to enable the proper determination of the timeliness of the execution of the contracts or project implementation.
We further recommended that the LCE instruct the Municipal Accountant to thoroughly review all supporting documents before payment is made and compute claims on liquidated damages for projects which incurred unwarranted delays in implementation.
We also recommended that the LCE instruct the Municipal Engineer to closely monitor the status of implementation of the projects, whether it is in accordance with the specifications set forth in the contract and the program of works, as well as the timeliness of the execution to avoid unnecessary delays in completion. Pictures should also be attached to the vouchers to support the percentage of completion reflected in the statement of work accomplished as submitted by the contractor which should be properly labeled and should display the implementation per item of work to enable proper verification thereof.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the MDRRMO-Interim and the Municipal Accountant to adhere strictly with the provision of Section 12 (c) of RA 10121  and Item 5.1.5 of COA Circular 2012-002 and submit regularly to the COA Audit Team of the municipality the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund, on or before the 15th day after the end of each month, through the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council and the Municipal Development Council.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Local Disaster Risk Management Officer and Municipal Budget Officer to properly charge the disbursements made against LDRRMF in accordance with DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 and other related guidelines on the utilization of the LDRRM Fund, and prioritize the projects and programs which address disaster prevention, preparedness, response, rehabilitation and recovery.
We also recommended that budgetary requirements in the creation and operations of the LDRRM Office be properly allocated.

We recommended that Local Chief Executive and the LDRRMO formulate feasible plans for PPAs to be included in the municipality’s annual LDRRMPs and LDRRMIPs to maximize the utilization of the allocation for LDRRM Program, Projects and Activities. Moreover, we recommended that only priority projects be included in the DRRM programs that are congruent to LDRRM guidelines and objectives. Lastly, we also recommended that allocation for the 70% LDRRMF should include the thematic areas of Disaster Response and Disaster Rehabilitation and Recovery programs.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and the Solid Waste Management Board hasten the preparation of its 10-year Ecological Solid Waste Management Plan and the closure and rehabilitation plan of its dumpsite to effect the eventual closure of the dumpsite and to comply with the requirements of Sections 16 and 37 of RA 9003.
We also recommended that the Solid Waste Management Board ensure that the municipal MRF be properly maintained and should be designed to accommodate efficient and safe materials processing, movement, and storage in accordance with Sections 32 and 33 of the same Act.
We further recommended that the Local Chief Executive and the Solid Waste Management Board improve its comprehensive workplan for SWM operation to include the corresponding budget and fund source per activity and the strategies/procedures to implement said activities. We also recommended that said workplan be incorporated in the AIP of the municipality to ensure its funding.
We also recommended that the SWMB enact and enforce laws and ordinances relative to the implementation of activities under the SWM Plan and establish proper management and monitoring mechanisms to ensure its efficient and effective implementation.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
a. 	Direct the Municipal Treasurer to avail of the remedies by administrative action to enforce payment on delinquent Real Property Taxes thru levy on real property or by judicial action as provided in Section 256 of RA 7160.
Direct the concerned officials and employees to conduct a tax campaign to inform taxpayers of the consequences of non-payment of property taxes.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the GAD Focal Person the immediate preparation and submission of the GAD accomplishment report for evaluation of the implementation of the GAD programs and activities, and strictly observe the provisions of the PCW, DILG, DBM and NEDA JMC No. 2016-01.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant effect the immediate submission of the lacking paid disbursement vouchers and supporting documents in compliance with the aforesaid laws, rules and regulations for timely audit and/or verification of the municipality’s financial transactions.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant maintain a special account for the 20% MDF and prepare the required financial statements as enumerated in the above mentioned guidelines in order to provide a comprehensive information as to the extent of implementation of programs/projects undertaken by the municipality that was sourced from its development fund.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the concerned PHWs to refund the overpayment of hazard pay amounting to ₱87,091.75 for the CY 2016, and that the Municipal Accountant comply with the prescribed guidelines under RA 7305 and COA Circular No. 2012-001 for the payment of hazard pay.

We recommended that the Municipal GAD Focal Point System make sure that the HGDG test be administered before the preparation of its GAD Plan and Budget as well as, in the Accomplishment Report to ensure that gender issues and the related activities included in the budget were properly identified. Also, the actions taken by the municipality on the COA GAD related audit findings and recommendations shall be attached to the GAD AR to enable proper evaluation of the reported accomplishments for the year.

We recommended that the Local Sanggunian through the Committee on Women and Family spearhead the formulation of the GAD Code in collaboration with the LGU offices/departments while the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) thru the MPDO shall spearhead the setting up and maintenance of the GAD Database. The GAD Focal Point System shall provide the necessary support to the Sanggunian and the MPDO for the said purpose.

We recommended that all Municipal Officials and Employees cause the refund of the PEI amounting to ₱3,048,467.58 in excess of its PS limitation granted to its employees since it is contrary to the provisions of Section 325 (a) of RA 7160 and Local Budget Circular No. 98.
We also recommended that the LGU management initiate and optimize the generation of resources and revenues, and ensure that all taxes and other revenues of the LGU are collected in order to increase the allowable PS level. Pertinent thereto, the Sangguniang Bayan should enact the corresponding ordinances levying taxes, fees and charges and prescribe the rates thereof and should come up with judicious and holistic evaluation of the reasonableness of projected revenues and other receipts.
We likewise recommended that the Municipal Finance Committee should set realizable collection targets in order to arrive at a doable budget that would respond to the actual needs of the Municipality.

We recommended the refund of the excess amount of Productivity Enhancement Incentive for CY 2013 totaling ₱3,274,000.00 paid to the Municipal officials and employees.
We also recommended that the management see to it that payments for allowances and other personnel benefits should be based on laws, rules and regulations authorizing such payments to avoid the suspension and disallowance of the amount.

We recommended that management submit all contracts, purchase orders and the like entered into, irrespective of the amount, to the Auditor within five (5) working days upon execution and issuance thereof.

We recommended that management see to it that the trip tickets of all vehicles used for official business by the Municipality are accomplished properly. All information needed in accomplishing a trip ticket should be provided as required in the trip ticket form in order that the purpose of the trip and fuel consumption could be established.

We recommended that the Chief Local Executive require officials concerned to see to it that disbursement vouchers are attached with proper and complete documents to establish the validity or veracity of the claims. Adherence to documentary requirements prescribed under existing accounting and auditing rules and regulations should be strictly observed.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) take the following courses of action:

1. Require the Inventory Committee to take initial steps to conduct the physical count of the property, plant and equipment, prepare the Report on the Physical Count of PPE (RPCPPE) and submit the same to the Auditor concerned not later than January 31 of each year.

2. Instruct the General Services Officer/Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to prepare the property stock cards and ledger cards, respectively and reconcile them with other property records like Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment (ARE).

3. Secure the titles of land owned and ensure that donated lands are transferred to the municipality.

We also recommended that that management see to it that payments for allowances and other personnel benefits should be based on laws, rules and regulations authorizing such payments to avoid the suspension and disallowance of the amount.

We recommended that management submit all contracts, purchase orders and the like entered into, irrespective of the amount, to the Auditor within five (5) working days upon execution and issuance thereof.

We recommended that management see to it that the trip tickets of all vehicles used for official business by the municipality are accomplished properly. All information needed in accomplishing a trip ticket should be provided as required in the trip ticket form in order that the purpose of the trip and fuel consumption could be established.

We recommended that the Chief Local Executive require officials concerned to see to it that disbursement vouchers are attached with proper and complete documents to establish the validity or veracity of the claims.  Adherence to documentary requirements prescribed under existing accounting and auditing rules and regulations should be strictly observed.

We recommend that the Local Chief Executive require all concerned officers and employees to liquidate their long outstanding cash advances and strictly adhere to the aforementioned provisions of Section 89 of PD No. 1445 and pertinent provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 and COA Circular No. 2012-004 to ensure proper granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances.

We also recommend the immediate refund of all unliquidated cash advances, otherwise, withhold the salaries of all officers and employees with outstanding cash advances until the amounts involved are fully restituted.

We recommend that the Municipality adhere to the guidelines on the localization of the Magna Carta of Women provided by the PCW, DILG, DBM and NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular 2013-01.

The Municipality should pursue its plan of having seminars/trainings to aid them in the preparation of the GAD Plan and Budget and the GAD Accomplishment Report.

We recommend that all Municipal Officials and Employees cause the refund of the PEI amounting to P3,048,467.58 in excess of its PS limitation granted to its employees since it is contrary to the provisions of Section 325 (a) of RA 7160 and Local Budget Circular No. 98.

We also recommend that the LGU management initiate and optimize the generation of resources and revenues, and ensure that all taxes and other revenues of the LGU are collected in order to increase the allowable PS level.
